incident_details.who_committed_the_alleged_discrimination: Santa Fe Trails Route 2 bus driver (eastbound) at Cerrillos & Zia who refused to deploy the ramp and denied me boarding
incident_details.location_of_the_discrimination_if_applic: Santa Fe Trails Route 2 stop at Cerrillos Road & Zia Road in Santa Fe, New Mexico (eastbound, near CHRISTUS St. Vincent)
incident_details.detailed_description_of_complaint_grieva:
I am a wheelchair user with a spinal cord injury and a high fall risk. Dr. Robert Klein at the CHRISTUS St. Vincent rehab outpatient clinic wrote in February 2022 that I should avoid prolonged unsupervised walking with a cane because of my fall risk, so my wheelchair and the Santa Fe Trails buses are my primary way of getting around.

On October 28, 2025, after a physical therapy appointment at CHRISTUS St. Vincent, I was traveling home using Santa Fe Trails Route 2. At approximately 4:15 p.m., at the eastbound Route 2 stop at Cerrillos & Zia, I positioned my wheelchair at the wheelchair symbol and clearly indicated that I needed the ramp by pointing to my chair and saying, “Ramp please.”

The Route 2 driver looked directly at my wheelchair and stated that he did not have time to “mess with the straps and all that” because he was running late. He refused to deploy the ramp, did not board me, closed the doors, and drove away in front of other passengers, leaving me on the curb despite my visible mobility impairment and use of a wheelchair.

Because of this denial of boarding, I had to wait almost 30 minutes for the next bus. By the time I was finally able to board a later bus, I missed my transfer at the Downtown Transit Center and did not get home until after 6:30 p.m. I ended up sitting in my wheelchair for over two hours in the cold.

The prolonged time in the chair caused severe neuropathic pain. I had already been on a low-dose oxycodone 5 mg and a nighttime muscle relaxant regimen prescribed by Dr. Karen Patel at the CHRISTUS Spine & Pain Center in March 2024. That evening, because of the pain from being stuck in the chair so long, I had to take an extra oxycodone 5 mg tablet on top of my usual dose and the muscle relaxant. I later emailed Dr. Patel’s office with the subject line “Refill request – oxycodone 5mg before next PT visit” because incidents like this make me go through my medication faster.

I also missed my 6:00 p.m. online chronic pain and trauma support group run by Melissa Gonzales, LCSW, through CHRISTUS Behavioral Health, which I have been attending every Tuesday since August 2023. That group specifically helps me manage panic attacks that are triggered when bus ramps are not deployed, so missing it on the night of this incident was especially harmful.

Emotionally, the incident was devastating. After finally reaching the Downtown Transit Center, I went into the women’s restroom, parked my chair in a corner stall, and cried for almost 10 minutes. I remember thinking, “Maybe I shouldn’t go out alone anymore if this is how people see me,” which was frightening to realize about myself. Being left on the curb because of my wheelchair made me feel like I did not belong in public spaces.

The incident also disrupted my children’s evening. Around 4:45 p.m., from the CHRISTUS parking lot, I had to text my daughter Sofi and ask her to skip her Santa Fe High band rehearsal for the November regional performance and go straight home to watch my son Diego, which also meant he missed his soccer practice. One driver’s decision not to deploy the ramp rippled through our entire family’s schedule.

Because of my financial situation—living on SSDI, a structured settlement, and part-time work—taking a rideshare from CHRISTUS to home is often not realistic. I cannot simply “walk home” due to the medical restrictions on unsupervised walking with a cane. This means that when a driver refuses to board me, I am effectively stranded. In this case, I was denied access to the bus system because of my disability-related need for a ramp and securement, which I believe is discrimination under the ADA.
incident_details.proposed_resolution_of_grievance:
To resolve this grievance and help prevent similar incidents in the future, I am requesting the following actions from the City of Santa Fe and the Transit Division:

1. Formal Investigation and Written Response:
   - Review the operational data for Route 2 on October 28, 2025, around 4:15 p.m., including driver assignments, GPS logs, and any available on-board camera footage for the eastbound bus at Cerrillos & Zia.
   - Identify the driver involved and evaluate his conduct against ADA requirements and City transit policies regarding riders who use wheelchairs and other mobility devices.
   - Provide me with a written explanation of your findings, including whether the driver’s actions violated ADA obligations or internal policy.

2. Driver Retraining and Corrective Action:
   - If the investigation confirms that I was improperly denied boarding, ensure that the driver receives documented training on ADA requirements, including the duty to stop for riders with mobility devices and to deploy the ramp and securement equipment when requested.
   - Take appropriate corrective or disciplinary action to emphasize the seriousness of denying access to riders with disabilities.

3. System-wide ADA Training:
   - Provide annual ADA refresher training for all transit staff, including drivers, supervisors, and dispatchers.
   - Incorporate real-life scenarios involving wheelchair users and other riders with mobility impairments.
   - Involve local disability advocacy groups or people with disabilities in the design or delivery of this training to ensure it reflects lived experience.

4. Policy Review and Communication:
   - Review and clarify written policies regarding boarding riders who use wheelchairs and other mobility devices, including ramp deployment and securement procedures.
   - Make these policies easily accessible on the City and transit websites.
   - Consider posting clear signage on buses and at major stops explaining riders’ rights under the ADA and the obligation of drivers to deploy ramps and securement equipment.

5. Service Recovery:
   - Provide a modest service recovery, such as a monthly pass credit, in recognition of the time, physical pain, and disruption caused by being denied boarding on October 28, 2025.

6. Follow-up Communication and Ongoing Input:
   - Have an ADA or Transit coordinator follow up with me by email (my preferred method) to explain what steps have been taken in response to this grievance.
   - I am open to participating in a meeting or conversation to share broader experiences as a wheelchair user on Santa Fe Trails and to provide feedback on improving system accessibility so that no one else is left on the curb because of their disability.

incident_details.who_committed_the_alleged_discrimination: City of Santa Fe Transit Division – Santa Fe Trails Bus System, Route 2 driver on the 4:15 p.m. eastbound bus (male driver, approximately mid‑50s, short gray hair, glasses, wearing City of Santa Fe Transit uniform)
incident_details.location_of_the_discrimination_if_applic: Onboard Santa Fe Trails Route 2 bus, stop at Cerrillos Rd & Zia Rd, continuing to Downtown Transit Center, Santa Fe, NM
incident_details.detailed_description_of_complaint_grieva:
I am a 47‑year‑old Santa Fe resident with a permanent mobility impairment due to a spinal cord injury from a car accident in 2016. I primarily use a manual wheelchair and occasionally a cane for very short distances. I rely heavily on the Santa Fe Trails bus system for getting to medical appointments, physical therapy, and my part‑time work at a local nonprofit.

On Tuesday, 2025-10-28, I attempted to board the Route 2 Santa Fe Trails bus at approximately 4:15 p.m. at the stop located at Cerrillos Rd & Zia Rd (eastbound side). I was returning from a physical therapy appointment at CHRISTUS St. Vincent and heading to the Downtown Transit Center to transfer to another route to get home.

When the bus arrived, I positioned myself in front of the marked wheelchair boarding area at the stop, clearly visible. The bus pulled up, and I could see that there were open wheelchair securement spaces inside—at least one, possibly two. As the front door opened, I signaled to the driver that I needed the ramp by pointing to my wheelchair and saying, "Ramp please." The driver looked directly at me, shook his head slightly, and said, "You’ll have to wait for the next one, I’m running behind." He did not attempt to deploy the ramp or even check the securement area properly.

I responded, as calmly as I could, "I really need to get to my connecting bus downtown. There’s space; can you please put the ramp down?" The driver sighed loudly in a way that felt dismissive and said, "Lady, I don’t have time to mess with the straps and all that. It takes too long, and we’re already late. Another bus will be along." He then gestured vaguely down the street, closed the doors, and pulled away, leaving me at the stop.

This interaction left me shocked and embarrassed. There were at least five other passengers at the stop, and two of them looked at me sympathetically and one said, "That’s not right, he’s supposed to let you on." I ended up waiting almost 30 more minutes for the next Route 2 bus. Because of the delay, I missed my transfer at the Downtown Transit Center and had to wait an additional hour for the next bus home. I did not get home until after 6:30 p.m., which is more than two hours after I left my appointment, even though the trip normally takes about 40–45 minutes.

This incident is not only inconvenient; I believe it is discriminatory under the ADA because:

1. I was denied access to a city transit service that is open to the general public specifically because of the extra time and effort the driver believed my disability would require. The driver’s exact words—"I don’t have time to mess with the straps and all that"—clearly refer to the accommodations required for my wheelchair.

2. The driver did not check to see if the securement area was actually unavailable. From what I could see through the window, there was at least one open wheelchair space. No one was occupying the priority seating area. The driver did not ask anyone to move or even look back there; he simply decided not to board me.

3. The tone and manner of the driver’s comments made me feel singled out and like a burden. It felt as if my need for an accessible boarding process was an inconvenience that justified denying me service. This is humiliating and contributes to my anxiety about using public transit, which is my primary mode of transportation.

4. This is not the first time I have felt that drivers are reluctant to deploy the ramp or take the time to secure my wheelchair, but this is the first time I have been flatly refused boarding. On previous occasions, drivers have made comments such as, "You’re really slowing us down," or "You know we’re going to be late now," while securing my chair. I have generally tried to ignore these remarks, but this most recent incident crossed a line because I was actually left behind.

Because of my disability, I cannot simply "wait for the next bus" without consequences. On this particular day, by the time I finally got home, I was in significant pain from sitting in my chair for so long and from the cold weather. I also missed a scheduled 6:00 p.m. online support group meeting that I attend weekly, which is important to my mental health. In addition, I had to call my teenage daughter and ask her to postpone her after‑school activity because I was not home in time to supervise her younger brother.

I want to emphasize that I generally find many of the Santa Fe Trails drivers to be courteous and helpful, and I appreciate the service overall. However, this incident demonstrates a serious problem with consistency in training and enforcement of ADA requirements. On some days, drivers are proactive and kind about using the ramp and securement system; on other days, they act like they are doing me a personal favor instead of fulfilling a legal obligation. Being refused boarding altogether is unacceptable and has made me feel unsafe and unwelcome using a service that I depend on.

I am filing this grievance because I want the City of Santa Fe to be aware that this is happening and to take concrete steps to ensure that people with disabilities are not denied equal access to public transportation. I am willing to provide additional details, including the exact time from my phone records, my bus pass data if helpful, and a more detailed description of the driver, if needed. I also have notes I wrote immediately after the incident in the notes app on my phone because I was so upset and didn’t want to forget the driver’s words.

To summarize: on 2025-10-28 at approximately 4:15 p.m., on Santa Fe Trails Route 2 at Cerrillos & Zia, I was denied boarding by the driver because of the extra time and effort he believed it would take to accommodate my wheelchair, even though there appeared to be space available. His comments and actions resulted in a significant delay, physical discomfort, missed commitments, and emotional distress, and I believe they constitute discrimination on the basis of disability under the ADA.
incident_details.proposed_resolution_of_grievance:
I am requesting several specific actions from the City of Santa Fe to resolve this grievance and to help prevent similar incidents from happening to me or to other riders with disabilities in the future:

1. **Formal investigation and written response:**
   - I would like the Transit Division to review any available data for Route 2 on 2025-10-28 around 4:15 p.m., including driver assignments, GPS logs, and any on‑board camera footage, if available.
   - Please identify the driver involved and review his conduct in light of ADA requirements and City policies.
   - I would like a written explanation of your findings and whether the driver’s actions were found to be in violation of policy.

2. **Driver counseling, retraining, and appropriate corrective action:**
   - I am asking that the driver who refused to board me receive specific, documented training or retraining on ADA requirements, including:
     * The obligation to stop for riders using mobility devices when it is safe to do so.
     * The requirement to deploy the ramp or lift and provide assistance as needed.
     * The requirement to use securement equipment and not refuse service because of the time it takes.
     * Appropriate, respectful communication with riders with disabilities.
   - If your investigation confirms that I was improperly denied boarding, I believe some form of corrective or disciplinary action is appropriate so that the seriousness of this issue is clear.

3. **System‑wide ADA refresher training for all transit staff:**
   - Based on my experiences over the past few years, I do not think this is an isolated problem. I request that the Transit Division provide a system‑wide ADA refresher training for all drivers and relevant staff at least annually.
   - This training should include real‑life scenarios involving wheelchair users and other riders with disabilities, emphasizing that accommodating us is not optional or dependent on schedule convenience.
   - If possible, involve local disability advocacy groups or individuals with disabilities in designing or presenting this training so drivers can hear directly from the community affected.

4. **Clear policy communication and signage:**
   - I would like the City to review and, if necessary, clarify its written policies on boarding riders with mobility devices and make those policies easily accessible online.
   - Consider posting clear signage on buses and at major stops stating that riders using wheelchairs and other mobility devices have the right to board and to reasonable accommodation under the ADA. This not only reminds drivers but also empowers riders to know their rights.

5. **Service recovery for this incident:**
   - While my primary concern is preventing this from happening again, I am also requesting a modest service recovery as acknowledgment of the harm caused. This could be in the form of a monthly pass credit or similar fare compensation, as I lost over two hours of my time, experienced physical pain from the extended travel, and missed an important support meeting.

6. **Follow‑up communication and option for meeting:**
   - I would appreciate a follow‑up phone call or email from an ADA or Transit coordinator to discuss what steps have been taken in response to my complaint.
   - If possible, I would welcome the opportunity to participate in a short virtual or in‑person meeting to share my broader experiences as a rider with a disability and to provide feedback on how the system can be more accessible and respectful.

My goal is not to get anyone in trouble for the sake of it, but to ensure that no one else in a wheelchair or with another disability is left on the curb and made to feel like a burden because accommodating them might make the bus run a few minutes late. I am asking the City of Santa Fe to take this complaint seriously, to treat it as an opportunity to improve training and accountability, and to communicate clearly back to me about what actions are taken.

differences.ground_truth:
I am a 47‑year‑old Santa Fe resident with a permanent mobility impairment due to a spinal cord injury from a car accident in 2016. I primarily use a manual wheelchair and occasionally a cane for very short distances. I rely heavily on the Santa Fe Trails bus system for getting to medical appointments, physical therapy, and my part‑time work at a local nonprofit.

On Tuesday, 2025-10-28, I attempted to board the Route 2 Santa Fe Trails bus at approximately 4:15 p.m. at the stop located at Cerrillos Rd & Zia Rd (eastbound side). I was returning from a physical therapy appointment at CHRISTUS St. Vincent and heading to the Downtown Transit Center to transfer to another route to get home.

When the bus arrived, I positioned myself in front of the marked wheelchair boarding area at the stop, clearly visible. The bus pulled up, and I could see that there were open wheelchair securement spaces inside—at least one, possibly two. As the front door opened, I signaled to the driver that I needed the ramp by pointing to my wheelchair and saying, "Ramp please." The driver looked directly at me, shook his head slightly, and said, "You’ll have to wait for the next one, I’m running behind." He did not attempt to deploy the ramp or even check the securement area properly.

I responded, as calmly as I could, "I really need to get to my connecting bus downtown. There’s space; can you please put the ramp down?" The driver sighed loudly in a way that felt dismissive and said, "Lady, I don’t have time to mess with the straps and all that. It takes too long, and we’re already late. Another bus will be along." He then gestured vaguely down the street, closed the doors, and pulled away, leaving me at the stop.

This interaction left me shocked and embarrassed. There were at least five other passengers at the stop, and two of them looked at me sympathetically and one said, "That’s not right, he’s supposed to let you on." I ended up waiting almost 30 more minutes for the next Route 2 bus. Because of the delay, I missed my transfer at the Downtown Transit Center and had to wait an additional hour for the next bus home. I did not get home until after 6:30 p.m., which is more than two hours after I left my appointment, even though the trip normally takes about 40–45 minutes.

This incident is not only inconvenient; I believe it is discriminatory under the ADA because:

1. I was denied access to a city transit service that is open to the general public specifically because of the extra time and effort the driver believed my disability would require. The driver’s exact words—"I don’t have time to mess with the straps and all that"—clearly refer to the accommodations required for my wheelchair.

2. The driver did not check to see if the securement area was actually unavailable. From what I could see through the window, there was at least one open wheelchair space. No one was occupying the priority seating area. The driver did not ask anyone to move or even look back there; he simply decided not to board me.

3. The tone and manner of the driver’s comments made me feel singled out and like a burden. It felt as if my need for an accessible boarding process was an inconvenience that justified denying me service. This is humiliating and contributes to my anxiety about using public transit, which is my primary mode of transportation.

4. This is not the first time I have felt that drivers are reluctant to deploy the ramp or take the time to secure my wheelchair, but this is the first time I have been flatly refused boarding. On previous occasions, drivers have made comments such as, "You’re really slowing us down," or "You know we’re going to be late now," while securing my chair. I have generally tried to ignore these remarks, but this most recent incident crossed a line because I was actually left behind.

Because of my disability, I cannot simply "wait for the next bus" without consequences. On this particular day, by the time I finally got home, I was in significant pain from sitting in my chair for so long and from the cold weather. I also missed a scheduled 6:00 p.m. online support group meeting that I attend weekly, which is important to my mental health. In addition, I had to call my teenage daughter and ask her to postpone her after‑school activity because I was not home in time to supervise her younger brother.

I want to emphasize that I generally find many of the Santa Fe Trails drivers to be courteous and helpful, and I appreciate the service overall. However, this incident demonstrates a serious problem with consistency in training and enforcement of ADA requirements. On some days, drivers are proactive and kind about using the ramp and securement system; on other days, they act like they are doing me a personal favor instead of fulfilling a legal obligation. Being refused boarding altogether is unacceptable and has made me feel unsafe and unwelcome using a service that I depend on.

I am filing this grievance because I want the City of Santa Fe to be aware that this is happening and to take concrete steps to ensure that people with disabilities are not denied equal access to public transportation. I am willing to provide additional details, including the exact time from my phone records, my bus pass data if helpful, and a more detailed description of the driver, if needed. I also have notes I wrote immediately after the incident in the notes app on my phone because I was so upset and didn’t want to forget the driver’s words.

To summarize: on 2025-10-28 at approximately 4:15 p.m., on Santa Fe Trails Route 2 at Cerrillos & Zia, I was denied boarding by the driver because of the extra time and effort he believed it would take to accommodate my wheelchair, even though there appeared to be space available. His comments and actions resulted in a significant delay, physical discomfort, missed commitments, and emotional distress, and I believe they constitute discrimination on the basis of disability under the ADA.
differences.generated:
I am a wheelchair user with a spinal cord injury and a high fall risk. Dr. Robert Klein at the CHRISTUS St. Vincent rehab outpatient clinic wrote in February 2022 that I should avoid prolonged unsupervised walking with a cane because of my fall risk, so my wheelchair and the Santa Fe Trails buses are my primary way of getting around.

On October 28, 2025, after a physical therapy appointment at CHRISTUS St. Vincent, I was traveling home using Santa Fe Trails Route 2. At approximately 4:15 p.m., at the eastbound Route 2 stop at Cerrillos & Zia, I positioned my wheelchair at the wheelchair symbol and clearly indicated that I needed the ramp by pointing to my chair and saying, “Ramp please.”

The Route 2 driver looked directly at my wheelchair and stated that he did not have time to “mess with the straps and all that” because he was running late. He refused to deploy the ramp, did not board me, closed the doors, and drove away in front of other passengers, leaving me on the curb despite my visible mobility impairment and use of a wheelchair.

Because of this denial of boarding, I had to wait almost 30 minutes for the next bus. By the time I was finally able to board a later bus, I missed my transfer at the Downtown Transit Center and did not get home until after 6:30 p.m. I ended up sitting in my wheelchair for over two hours in the cold.

The prolonged time in the chair caused severe neuropathic pain. I had already been on a low-dose oxycodone 5 mg and a nighttime muscle relaxant regimen prescribed by Dr. Karen Patel at the CHRISTUS Spine & Pain Center in March 2024. That evening, because of the pain from being stuck in the chair so long, I had to take an extra oxycodone 5 mg tablet on top of my usual dose and the muscle relaxant. I later emailed Dr. Patel’s office with the subject line “Refill request – oxycodone 5mg before next PT visit” because incidents like this make me go through my medication faster.

I also missed my 6:00 p.m. online chronic pain and trauma support group run by Melissa Gonzales, LCSW, through CHRISTUS Behavioral Health, which I have been attending every Tuesday since August 2023. That group specifically helps me manage panic attacks that are triggered when bus ramps are not deployed, so missing it on the night of this incident was especially harmful.

Emotionally, the incident was devastating. After finally reaching the Downtown Transit Center, I went into the women’s restroom, parked my chair in a corner stall, and cried for almost 10 minutes. I remember thinking, “Maybe I shouldn’t go out alone anymore if this is how people see me,” which was frightening to realize about myself. Being left on the curb because of my wheelchair made me feel like I did not belong in public spaces.

The incident also disrupted my children’s evening. Around 4:45 p.m., from the CHRISTUS parking lot, I had to text my daughter Sofi and ask her to skip her Santa Fe High band rehearsal for the November regional performance and go straight home to watch my son Diego, which also meant he missed his soccer practice. One driver’s decision not to deploy the ramp rippled through our entire family’s schedule.

Because of my financial situation—living on SSDI, a structured settlement, and part-time work—taking a rideshare from CHRISTUS to home is often not realistic. I cannot simply “walk home” due to the medical restrictions on unsupervised walking with a cane. This means that when a driver refuses to board me, I am effectively stranded. In this case, I was denied access to the bus system because of my disability-related need for a ramp and securement, which I believe is discrimination under the ADA.

project_description.describe_proposal_and_reason_necessary: Conversion and use of the existing garage at 1847 Bluff Street as a low‑impact home office and art studio. The space functions as a dedicated remote‑work office for the owner’s software engineering position, a private location for telehealth medical check‑ins, and a quiet area for evening online coursework. It also serves as an art studio for the owner’s spouse. The use is intended to remain accessory to the primary residence, not a separate dwelling unit or intensive commercial space. The dedicated studio/office is necessary to provide a quiet, private environment for high‑responsibility remote work and medical consultations, to separate work activities from family living areas, and to avoid the financial burden of renting outside office space, while maintaining compatibility with the surrounding residential neighborhood.

site_and_zoning_information.legal_description_sidwell_parcel_id_or_l: Lot 12 and the North 10 feet of Lot 13, Block 7, City of Dubuque, Iowa, according to the Original Plat thereof, now included in and forming a part of the City of Dubuque, Dubuque County, Iowa. Parcel ID: 10-03-31-234-012.

project_description.describe_proposal_and_reason_necessary:
I am requesting approval of a Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan review to allow the conversion of my existing detached two-stall garage at 1847 Bluff Street into a small, accessory home office and artist studio, while maintaining one enclosed parking stall and one paved outdoor parking space. The primary use of the property will remain a single-family residence, which I, Daniel Whitaker, occupy as my full-time home.

Existing Conditions:
The property is a 2.5-story wood-frame home constructed circa 1890, located within the Bluff Historic Preservation District on Bluff Street between 18th and 19th Streets. The lot slopes gently from east (alley) down toward Bluff Street. The existing detached garage is located at the rear (east) of the lot, accessed via the public alley. The garage is approximately 22' x 24' (about 528 sq ft) with a low-pitch gable roof, wood lap siding, and an existing concrete slab. There is currently no plumbing in the garage; it has minimal electrical service (one light and one receptacle) and is used primarily for storage and parking one vehicle.

Proposed Project:
The proposal is to partially convert the interior of the garage into a small office and studio space (approximately 320 sq ft) while preserving one standard parking stall inside the structure and one additional parking stall on the existing concrete pad immediately west of the overhead door. The office/studio will be used by me for my work as a remote software engineer and by my spouse, who is an amateur watercolor painter. No employees will work on-site, and no customers or clients will visit the property on a regular basis.

The scope of work includes:
- Framing an insulated wall to separate the remaining garage bay from the new office/studio space.
- Installing code-compliant insulation, drywall, and finishes in the office/studio area to create a comfortable, year-round workspace.
- Adding two operable wood-clad windows on the south elevation of the garage to provide natural light and egress, with trim and proportions to match the existing house and neighboring accessory structures.
- Replacing the current non-historic metal overhead door with a wood carriage-style overhead door that is more compatible with the historic character of the district.
- Upgrading electrical service to provide additional outlets, dedicated circuits for computer equipment, and energy-efficient LED lighting.
- Installing a small, high-efficiency electric mini-split system (heating and cooling) to serve only the office/studio portion of the building.
- Adding a compact half-bathroom (toilet and sink only) within the office/studio, with new plumbing tied into existing sanitary and water service lines on the property in accordance with City and State plumbing codes.
- Maintaining and slightly widening the existing paved parking pad off the alley to ensure one full off-street parking space remains available in addition to the interior garage stall.

Reason the Request is Necessary:
Under the current R-2 Two-Family Residential zoning, a home occupation and accessory structure conversion of this nature is allowed only with a Conditional Use Permit and site plan review to ensure that the use remains clearly incidental to the primary residential use and does not adversely impact surrounding properties. Because I will be conducting my software engineering work from home on a full-time basis, and because the converted area will be finished space with a small restroom, City staff advised that a Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan are required rather than treating this purely as an interior remodel.

The request is necessary for several reasons:
1. Long-Term Remote Work: Since 2020, my employer (a mid-sized health technology company based in Minneapolis) has transitioned to a permanent remote-work model. I am under a long-term employment agreement that specifically allows and encourages remote work from home. Working from the dining room table and spare bedroom has created ongoing challenges with noise, ergonomics, and separation of work and personal life. Having a dedicated, code-compliant office space in the converted garage will allow me to continue my current employment without needing to rent commercial office space.

2. Reduced Commuting and Environmental Impact: Prior to working remotely, I commuted by car approximately 35 miles round-trip daily to an office in Peosta, Iowa. That commute contributed to traffic and emissions. By formalizing a home-based workspace, I can permanently avoid that commute, thereby reducing vehicle trips, fuel consumption, and associated emissions in a way that aligns with the Citys sustainability goals.

3. Compatibility with Neighborhood Character: The proposed use is quiet, low-intensity, and fully contained within an existing accessory structure at the rear of the property. No exterior changes are proposed that would alter the residential character of the home or the streetscape along Bluff Street. The only visible changes from the alley will be the upgraded overhead door, two modestly scaled windows, and minor site improvements such as landscaping and the slight widening of the existing parking pad. No signage is proposed, no outdoor storage will be allowed, and there will be no regular customer or client traffic. Typical daily activity will consist of my walking from the main house to the garage office in the morning, working on a computer for most of the day, and occasionally stepping outside to take phone calls or let our dog out.

4. Preservation and Enhancement of Existing Structures: The detached garage is currently in fair but aging condition. Without this project, we would likely continue to use it primarily as storage, and it would gradually deteriorate. By investing in structural repairs, new windows, siding repairs, and improved roofing, we will prolong the life of the building and improve its overall appearance in a manner consistent with the Bluff Historic Preservation District guidelines. We have already consulted with a local architect familiar with historic design standards to ensure that window proportions, trim details, and materials will be compatible with the surrounding historic context.

5. Personal and Family Considerations: My spouse, Anna, works part-time as a substitute teacher and spends her remaining time painting watercolors and illustrating childrens books on a freelance basis. She currently paints at a small table in our living room, which is not ideal for ventilation or for keeping materials secure from our two young children (ages 5 and 8). The converted studio will provide a safe, organized, and well-ventilated space for her art supplies, along with storage for completed canvases and prints. This will improve safety for our children and reduce clutter in our main living areas.

Operational Details and Commitments:
- Hours of Use: My typical work hours will be 8:00 a.m. to 5:30 p.m., Monday through Friday, with occasional evening or weekend work during software releases. Anna will use the studio intermittently, usually mid-morning or early afternoon, no later than 8:00 p.m.
- Employees: There will be no employees working on-site other than myself. All collaboration with coworkers is virtual, conducted by video conference, chat, and email.
- Customer/Client Traffic: I do not meet with clients in person as part of my job. On rare occasions (anticipated no more than 3	6 times per year), a coworker or project stakeholder may visit for a brief meeting. These visits will be pre-scheduled during normal daytime hours, and visitors will typically park on Bluff Street for a short duration, similar to a normal residential guest.
- Deliveries: I receive occasional small-package deliveries (e.g., computer equipment, office supplies, and art materials) via standard parcel services (USPS, UPS, FedEx). Delivery frequency is approximately 1	3 packages per week, which is comparable to any modern household and will not create additional traffic beyond what is typical for the neighborhood.
- Noise and Odors: The primary office use is computer-based and produces negligible noise beyond normal conversation levels. Anna uses water-based paints and non-toxic materials; no industrial processes, power tools, or heavy equipment will be used in the studio. We will not use oil-based solvents or spray finishes that could generate noticeable odors.
- Parking: The site will continue to provide two off-street parking spaces accessed from the alley (one interior, one exterior), which is consistent with or better than many neighboring properties. Our household owns two vehicles, and we will prioritize parking off-street whenever feasible. The project does not reduce the number of available off-street spaces.

In summary, the requested Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan approval will allow us to make a modest, context-sensitive improvement to an existing accessory structure, support long-term remote employment and a small-scale artistic practice, and preserve and enhance the historic character of our property and the surrounding district. The proposed use is clearly subordinate to the primary residential use, will not generate significant traffic, noise, or visual impacts, and is designed to meet all applicable zoning, building, and historic design standards.

- Upgrading electrical service to provide additional outlets, dedicated circuits for computer equipment, and energy-efficient LED lighting.
- Installing a small, high-efficiency electric mini-split system (heating and cooling) to serve only the office/studio portion of the building.
- Adding a compact half-bathroom (toilet and sink only) within the office/studio, with new plumbing tied into existing sanitary and water service lines on the property in accordance with City and State plumbing codes.
- Maintaining and slightly widening the existing paved parking pad off the alley to ensure one full off-street parking space remains available in addition to the interior garage stall.

Reason the Request is Necessary:
Under the current R-2 Two-Family Residential zoning, a home occupation and accessory structure conversion of this nature is allowed only with a Conditional Use Permit and site plan review to ensure that the use remains clearly incidental to the primary residential use and does not adversely impact surrounding properties. Because I will be conducting my software engineering work from home on a full-time basis, and because the converted area will be finished space with a small restroom, City staff advised that a Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan are required rather than treating this purely as an interior remodel.

The request is necessary for several reasons:
1. Long-Term Remote Work: Since 2020, my employer (a mid-sized health technology company based in Minneapolis) has transitioned to a permanent remote-work model. I am under a long-term employment agreement that specifically allows and encourages remote work from home. Working from the dining room table and spare bedroom has created ongoing challenges with noise, ergonomics, and separation of work and personal life. Having a dedicated, code-compliant office space in the converted garage will allow me to continue my current employment without needing to rent commercial office space.

2. Reduced Commuting and Environmental Impact: Prior to working remotely, I commuted by car approximately 35 miles round-trip daily to an office in Peosta, Iowa. That commute contributed to traffic and emissions. By formalizing a home-based workspace, I can permanently avoid that commute, thereby reducing vehicle trips, fuel consumption, and associated emissions in a way that aligns with the Citys sustainability goals.

3. Compatibility with Neighborhood Character: The proposed use is quiet, low-intensity, and fully contained within an existing accessory structure at the rear of the property. No exterior changes are proposed that would alter the residential character of the home or the streetscape along Bluff Street. The only visible changes from the alley will be the upgraded overhead door, two modestly scaled windows, and minor site improvements such as landscaping and the slight widening of the existing parking pad. No signage is proposed, no outdoor storage will be allowed, and there will be no regular customer or client traffic. Typical daily activity will consist of my walking from the main house to the garage office in the morning, working on a computer for most of the day, and occasionally stepping outside to take phone calls or let our dog out.

4. Preservation and Enhancement of Existing Structures: The detached garage is currently in fair but aging condition. Without this project, we would likely continue to use it primarily as storage, and it would gradually deteriorate. By investing in structural repairs, new windows, siding repairs, and improved roofing, we will prolong the life of the building and improve its overall appearance in a manner consistent with the Bluff Historic Preservation District guidelines. We have already consulted with a local architect familiar with historic design standards to ensure that window proportions, trim details, and materials will be compatible with the surrounding historic context.

5. Personal and Family Considerations: My spouse, Anna, works part-time as a substitute teacher and spends her remaining time painting watercolors and illustrating childrens books on a freelance basis. She currently paints at a small table in our living room, which is not ideal for ventilation or for keeping materials secure from our two young children (ages 5 and 8). The converted studio will provide a safe, organized, and well-ventilated space for her art supplies, along with storage for completed canvases and prints. This will improve safety for our children and reduce clutter in our main living areas.

Operational Details and Commitments:
- Hours of Use: My typical work hours will be 8:00 a.m. to 5:30 p.m., Monday through Friday, with occasional evening or weekend work during software releases. Anna will use the studio intermittently, usually mid-morning or early afternoon, no later than 8:00 p.m.
- Employees: There will be no employees working on-site other than myself. All collaboration with coworkers is virtual, conducted by video conference, chat, and email.
- Customer/Client Traffic: I do not meet with clients in person as part of my job. On rare occasions (anticipated no more than 3	6 times per year), a coworker or project stakeholder may visit for a brief meeting. These visits will be pre-scheduled during normal daytime hours, and visitors will typically park on Bluff Street for a short duration, similar to a normal residential guest.
- Deliveries: I receive occasional small-package deliveries (e.g., computer equipment, office supplies, and art materials) via standard parcel services (USPS, UPS, FedEx). Delivery frequency is approximately 1	3 packages per week, which is comparable to any modern household and will not create additional traffic beyond what is typical for the neighborhood.
- Noise and Odors: The primary office use is computer-based and produces negligible noise beyond normal conversation levels. Anna uses water-based paints and non-toxic materials; no industrial processes, power tools, or heavy equipment will be used in the studio. We will not use oil-based solvents or spray finishes that could generate noticeable odors.
- Parking: The site will continue to provide two off-street parking spaces accessed from the alley (one interior, one exterior), which is consistent with or better than many neighboring properties. Our household owns two vehicles, and we will prioritize parking off-street whenever feasible. The project does not reduce the number of available off-street spaces.

In summary, the requested Conditional Use Permit and Simple Site Plan approval will allow us to make a modest, context-sensitive improvement to an existing accessory structure, support long-term remote employment and a small-scale artistic practice, and preserve and enhance the historic character of our property and the surrounding district. The proposed use is clearly subordinate to the primary residential use, will not generate significant traffic, noise, or visual impacts, and is designed to meet all applicable zoning, building, and historic design standards.

church__community_involvement.how_long_lived_in_hope_west_campus_prope: My family and I have lived in Hope West Campus housing for just under two years. We moved into the community in August 2023 when my dad changed jobs and we were looking for a more affordable and supportive neighborhood close to church and school. Since then, Hope West Campus has really become home for us. I have gotten to know several of our neighbors, especially the families with younger kids, and I often help watch their children when they need to run errands or attend evening Bible study. Living on the property has also made it easier for me to get involved in mid-week church activities, youth group, and volunteer projects because I can walk over to the church campus in less than ten minutes. Overall, our time here has been stable, positive, and has allowed my family to be much more plugged into the life of The Hope Church community.
church__community_involvement.describe_your_community_service_activiti:
Over the past several years, I have been intentional about serving consistently in both my church and the broader Columbus community, mainly focusing on youth, food insecurity, and neighborhood clean-up.

At The Hope Church, I volunteer twice a month with the children's ministry, specifically with the 3rd–5th grade Sunday class. I help set up the classroom, check in the kids, assist with small group discussions, and sometimes lead short activities or object lessons tied to the Bible story for that week. For example, one Sunday we were teaching about the story of the Good Samaritan, and I helped design a role-play activity where the kids practiced what it might look like to help someone who is left out at school or on the playground. Watching them act out these situations and then talk honestly about times when they had either helped or ignored someone in need was really powerful for me.

I also serve regularly with our church's monthly food pantry outreach. On the second Saturday of each month, I help unload the food truck, organize canned goods and produce into family-sized boxes, and then carry groceries out to people's cars. I try to greet each person with a smile, ask how they are doing, and take a moment to really listen if they want to talk. Over time, I have gotten to know a few of the regular families by name, and we will exchange updates about school, jobs, or health. This has helped me see that service is not just about handing out food, but about building relationships and showing people that they are seen and valued.

At school, I am a member of the Central City High School Community Service Club. Through this club, I have participated in several neighborhood clean-up days where we pick up trash along main streets, paint over graffiti (with permission from the city), and help plant flowers in public spaces like the library and community center. One project that meant a lot to me was helping to repaint and clean up the small playground at a nearby apartment complex where a lot of low-income families live. Some of the kids came out to watch us work, and by the end of the day they were playing on the swings and slides that we had just scrubbed down.

In addition, I volunteer as a peer tutor in our school's after-school tutoring program two days a week. I primarily help 9th and 10th grade students with algebra and biology. One of the students I worked with this year was really struggling with math and felt embarrassed to ask questions in class. We met regularly on Tuesdays, and I broke down problems step by step, using real-life examples like splitting pizza or tracking sports statistics. Over the semester, her quiz grades improved from the 60s into the low 80s, and she started raising her hand more in class. Seeing her confidence grow reminded me how impactful it can be just to give someone your time, patience, and encouragement.

Finally, during the summers, I have volunteered at a one-week Vacation Bible School at The Hope Church, helping lead game stations and snack time. This requires a lot of energy and patience, but it is also one of the most joyful weeks of my year. I get to work alongside adult volunteers and other teens, and we make a point to learn each child's name and something unique about them, so they feel personally known and welcomed.

Across all these activities, I have learned that community service is not just a requirement for a scholarship or a line on a college application. For me, it has become a way to live out my faith in everyday, practical ways—by showing up consistently, being reliable, and doing the small tasks that often go unnoticed but make a real difference in people's lives.

education_plans.planned_field_of_study: Associate of Applied Science in Information Technology – Cybersecurity Focus

required_attachments.awards_and_honors:
• National Honor Society – Inducted April 2024, Central City High School. Recognized for academic excellence (GPA above 3.5), leadership, and community service involvement (over 75 documented service hours).

• Honor Roll – Central City High School, 2021–2025. Achieved Honor Roll status every semester from 9th through 12th grade by maintaining a GPA above 3.5.

• STEM Excellence Award – May 2024, Central City High School Science Department. Received for outstanding performance in Honors Biology and Chemistry, consistent high lab report scores, and leadership in group projects.

• Church Youth Leadership Award – December 2023, The Hope Church. Recognized for consistent volunteer service in children's ministry, participation in youth leadership team, and reliability in supporting church outreach events.

• Community Service Recognition Certificate – May 2023, Central City High School Community Service Club. Awarded for completing over 120 verified community service hours during the 2022–2023 school year, including food pantry work, neighborhood clean-up, and peer tutoring.

• Local Tech Pathways Scholarship (one-time book scholarship) – Awarded March 2025 by Columbus Tech Forward Initiative, Amount: $500. Recognized for interest in pursuing a career in Information Technology and for completing an introductory coding workshop series.
required_attachments.personal_essay:
From the time I was young, I have been the person in my family who is called over whenever a phone freezes, a laptop will not connect to the internet, or the printer starts flashing mysterious error codes. What started as simple curiosity and a willingness to click around and figure out what was wrong slowly grew into a genuine passion for technology. Over the past few years, that interest has become more focused as I learned about cybersecurity and the ways that technology can be used both to protect and to harm people. I want to attend college so that I can gain the knowledge, training, and credentials I need to build a career in cybersecurity, helping protect individuals, small organizations, and churches like mine from online threats.

Academically, I have always enjoyed problem-solving and thinking logically, which is why math and science are my strongest subjects. In high school, I took every technology-related elective that was available to me, including an introductory coding class where we learned the basics of Python and JavaScript. I discovered that I love the feeling of working through a bug in my code and finally seeing the program run correctly. More recently, I participated in a weekend workshop sponsored by a local nonprofit where we were introduced to concepts like network security, phishing attacks, and password management. During that workshop, we simulated a phishing email scenario and analyzed the small details that can give away a scam—things like subtle spelling errors, suspicious links, and mismatched sender addresses. It opened my eyes to how vulnerable many people are online, especially those who are not very comfortable with technology.

My long-term career goal is to become a cybersecurity analyst or security engineer, focusing on helping small organizations and community-based institutions that may not have large IT departments. I think about places like my church, local nonprofits, and small businesses that store sensitive data but often cannot afford dedicated security staff. I want to be someone who can come alongside those organizations, assess their systems, identify vulnerabilities, and put protections in place so that they can continue to serve people without being derailed by data breaches or ransomware attacks. I am also interested in the educational side of cybersecurity—helping everyday people learn practical habits like creating strong passwords, recognizing scams, and safely using social media.

Attending Columbus State Community College is the first step in that journey for me. Their Information Technology program with a cybersecurity focus will allow me to build a solid foundation in networking, operating systems, and security principles. As a first-generation college student, I know I will face a learning curve in understanding how to navigate college life, from registering for classes to balancing school, work, and family responsibilities. However, I am motivated to work hard, seek out tutoring and advising when I need it, and take advantage of internship or co-op opportunities that will give me real-world experience.

This scholarship would make a meaningful difference for my family and me. My parents both work full-time—my mom as a medical assistant at a local clinic and my dad as a warehouse supervisor—but we have always lived on a careful budget. Over the past few years, unexpected medical bills and rising living costs have made it difficult for them to set aside money for college. While I plan to work part-time while attending school and to apply for federal financial aid, there is still a gap between what we can afford and the total cost of tuition, books, transportation, and basic living expenses.

Receiving this scholarship would help close that gap and reduce the amount of student loan debt I need to take on, allowing me to focus more on my studies and less on worrying about how every textbook will be paid for. It would also be a tangible sign to my family that our church community believes in me and is invested in my future. My younger siblings often look up to me, and being able to show them that college is possible, especially with the support of our church, is important to me. I want them to see that our circumstances do not have to limit our dreams when we are willing to work hard and ask for help.

My faith is at the center of why I am pursuing higher education and a career in cybersecurity. Growing up in The Hope Church, I was taught that our gifts and talents are not just for ourselves, but are meant to be used in service to others and to bring glory to God. For me, technology has always felt like one of those gifts. I believe that God can use me in this field to help protect people from harm, whether that harm is financial, emotional, or even spiritual. In a world where so much of our lives now happens online, I see cybersecurity as a form of stewardship—caring for the digital resources and personal information that have been entrusted to us.

There have been moments when I have questioned whether I am smart enough or prepared enough for a career in such a technical field. In those times, my faith has reminded me that my identity is not based solely on my grades or how quickly I pick up a new programming language. Instead, I am rooted in the belief that I am created with purpose, and that God can work through my efforts, even when I feel inadequate. Verses like James 1:5, which talks about asking God for wisdom, have encouraged me to pray not just for good test scores, but for clarity, perseverance, and integrity in how I approach my studies.

I also see education as a way to break cycles. My parents have always worked hard, but neither of them had the opportunity to complete a college degree. They have sacrificed a great deal to give me and my siblings a more stable life. By earning a degree and building a career in a growing field, I hope to contribute to my family's financial stability and to be in a position one day to give back—to my parents, to my church, and to other students who come after me. My dream is not just to succeed personally, but to reach a place where I can mentor younger students, especially those from similar backgrounds, and help them navigate their own paths into technology and higher education.

In summary, I want to attend college because I believe it is the next faithful step in developing the skills and knowledge I need to serve others through technology. My career goals in cybersecurity are driven by a desire to protect vulnerable people and organizations and to promote safety and integrity in the digital world. This scholarship would ease the financial burden on my family, allowing me to focus more fully on my education and to make the most of the opportunities ahead of me. Most importantly, my faith shapes how I view this entire journey—not as a path to personal status, but as a calling to use my abilities in a way that honors God and benefits my community.

differences.ground_truth:
Over the past several years, I have been intentional about serving consistently in both my church and the broader Columbus community, mainly focusing on youth, food insecurity, and neighborhood clean-up.

At The Hope Church, I volunteer twice a month with the children's ministry, specifically with the 3rd–5th grade Sunday class. I help set up the classroom, check in the kids, assist with small group discussions, and sometimes lead short activities or object lessons tied to the Bible story for that week. For example, one Sunday we were teaching about the story of the Good Samaritan, and I helped design a role-play activity where the kids practiced what it might look like to help someone who is left out at school or on the playground. Watching them act out these situations and then talk honestly about times when they had either helped or ignored someone in need was really powerful for me.

I also serve regularly with our church's monthly food pantry outreach. On the second Saturday of each month, I help unload the food truck, organize canned goods and produce into family-sized boxes, and then carry groceries out to people's cars. I try to greet each person with a smile, ask how they are doing, and take a moment to really listen if they want to talk. Over time, I have gotten to know a few of the regular families by name, and we will exchange updates about school, jobs, or health. This has helped me see that service is not just about handing out food, but about building relationships and showing people that they are seen and valued.

At school, I am a member of the Central City High School Community Service Club. Through this club, I have participated in several neighborhood clean-up days where we pick up trash along main streets, paint over graffiti (with permission from the city), and help plant flowers in public spaces like the library and community center. One project that meant a lot to me was helping to repaint and clean up the small playground at a nearby apartment complex where a lot of low-income families live. Some of the kids came out to watch us work, and by the end of the day they were playing on the swings and slides that we had just scrubbed down.

In addition, I volunteer as a peer tutor in our school's after-school tutoring program two days a week. I primarily help 9th and 10th grade students with algebra and biology. One of the students I worked with this year was really struggling with math and felt embarrassed to ask questions in class. We met regularly on Tuesdays, and I broke down problems step by step, using real-life examples like splitting pizza or tracking sports statistics. Over the semester, her quiz grades improved from the 60s into the low 80s, and she started raising her hand more in class. Seeing her confidence grow reminded me how impactful it can be just to give someone your time, patience, and encouragement.

Finally, during the summers, I have volunteered at a one-week Vacation Bible School at The Hope Church, helping lead game stations and snack time. This requires a lot of energy and patience, but it is also one of the most joyful weeks of my year. I get to work alongside adult volunteers and other teens, and we make a point to learn each child's name and something unique about them, so they feel personally known and welcomed.

Across all these activities, I have learned that community service is not just a requirement for a scholarship or a line on a college application. For me, it has become a way to live out my faith in everyday, practical ways—by showing up consistently, being reliable, and doing the small tasks that often go unnoticed but make a real difference in people's lives.

differences.ground_truth: My family and I have lived in Hope West Campus housing for just under two years. We moved into the community in August 2023 when my dad changed jobs and we were looking for a more affordable and supportive neighborhood close to church and school. Since then, Hope West Campus has really become home for us. I have gotten to know several of our neighbors, especially the families with younger kids, and I often help watch their children when they need to run errands or attend evening Bible study. Living on the property has also made it easier for me to get involved in mid-week church activities, youth group, and volunteer projects because I can walk over to the church campus in less than ten minutes. Overall, our time here has been stable, positive, and has allowed my family to be much more plugged into the life of The Hope Church community.

From the time I was young, I have been the person in my family who is called over whenever a phone freezes, a laptop will not connect to the internet, or the printer starts flashing mysterious error codes. What started as simple curiosity and a willingness to click around and figure out what was wrong slowly grew into a genuine passion for technology. Over the past few years, that interest has become more focused as I learned about cybersecurity and the ways that technology can be used both to protect and to harm people. I want to attend college so that I can gain the knowledge, training, and credentials I need to build a career in cybersecurity, helping protect individuals, small organizations, and churches like mine from online threats.

Academically, I have always enjoyed problem-solving and thinking logically, which is why math and science are my strongest subjects. In high school, I took every technology-related elective that was available to me, including an introductory coding class where we learned the basics of Python and JavaScript. I discovered that I love the feeling of working through a bug in my code and finally seeing the program run correctly. More recently, I participated in a weekend workshop sponsored by a local nonprofit where we were introduced to concepts like network security, phishing attacks, and password management. During that workshop, we simulated a phishing email scenario and analyzed the small details that can give away a scam—things like subtle spelling errors, suspicious links, and mismatched sender addresses. It opened my eyes to how vulnerable many people are online, especially those who are not very comfortable with technology.

My long-term career goal is to become a cybersecurity analyst or security engineer, focusing on helping small organizations and community-based institutions that may not have large IT departments. I think about places like my church, local nonprofits, and small businesses that store sensitive data but often cannot afford dedicated security staff. I want to be someone who can come alongside those organizations, assess their systems, identify vulnerabilities, and put protections in place so that they can continue to serve people without being derailed by data breaches or ransomware attacks. I am also interested in the educational side of cybersecurity—helping everyday people learn practical habits like creating strong passwords, recognizing scams, and safely using social media.

Attending Columbus State Community College is the first step in that journey for me. Their Information Technology program with a cybersecurity focus will allow me to build a solid foundation in networking, operating systems, and security principles. As a first-generation college student, I know I will face a learning curve in understanding how to navigate college life, from registering for classes to balancing school, work, and family responsibilities. However, I am motivated to work hard, seek out tutoring and advising when I need it, and take advantage of internship or co-op opportunities that will give me real-world experience.

This scholarship would make a meaningful difference for my family and me. My parents both work full-time—my mom as a medical assistant at a local clinic and my dad as a warehouse supervisor—but we have always lived on a careful budget. Over the past few years, unexpected medical bills and rising living costs have made it difficult for them to set aside money for college. While I plan to work part-time while attending school and to apply for federal financial aid, there is still a gap between what we can afford and the total cost of tuition, books, transportation, and basic living expenses.

Receiving this scholarship would help close that gap and reduce the amount of student loan debt I need to take on, allowing me to focus more on my studies and less on worrying about how every textbook will be paid for. It would also be a tangible sign to my family that our church community believes in me and is invested in my future. My younger siblings often look up to me, and being able to show them that college is possible, especially with the support of our church, is important to me. I want them to see that our circumstances do not have to limit our dreams when we are willing to work hard and ask for help.

My faith is at the center of why I am pursuing higher education and a career in cybersecurity. Growing up in The Hope Church, I was taught that our gifts and talents are not just for ourselves, but are meant to be used in service to others and to bring glory to God. For me, technology has always felt like one of those gifts. I believe that God can use me in this field to help protect people from harm, whether that harm is financial, emotional, or even spiritual. In a world where so much of our lives now happens online, I see cybersecurity as a form of stewardship—caring for the digital resources and personal information that have been entrusted to us.

There have been moments when I have questioned whether I am smart enough or prepared enough for a career in such a technical field. In those times, my faith has reminded me that my identity is not based solely on my grades or how quickly I pick up a new programming language. Instead, I am rooted in the belief that I am created with purpose, and that God can work through my efforts, even when I feel inadequate. Verses like James 1:5, which talks about asking God for wisdom, have encouraged me to pray not just for good test scores, but for clarity, perseverance, and integrity in how I approach my studies.

I also see education as a way to break cycles. My parents have always worked hard, but neither of them had the opportunity to complete a college degree. By earning a degree and building a career in a growing field, I hope to contribute to my family's financial stability and to be in a position one day to give back—to my parents, to my church, and to other students who come after me. My dream is not just to succeed personally, but to reach a place where I can mentor younger students, especially those from similar backgrounds, and help them navigate their own paths into technology and higher education.

In summary, I want to attend college because I believe it is the next faithful step in developing the skills and knowledge I need to serve others through technology. My career goals in cybersecurity are driven by a desire to protect vulnerable people and organizations and to promote safety and integrity in the digital world. achievements_and_justification.civic_involvement_if_known: Volunteers with the Peachtree Ridge CERT program and Habitat for Humanity in Gwinnett County, and serves on the Peachtree Ridge High School CTE Advisory Board engaging with students. Has also participated in evening Rotary talks related to building safety and public service.
achievements_and_justification.other_notable_achievements: Long-term leadership in the Metro Atlanta BOAG Chapter, serving as Treasurer (2008–2011), Vice President (2011–2014), President (2014–2017), and continuing as a Board Member Emeritus. Significant service to the Building Officials Association of Georgia (BOAG) as Board Director, Vice President, and President. Active involvement with ICC Region VIII as Georgia Representative and later Vice Chair. Contributions at the ICC level as a member of the ICC Code Development Committee and as an ICC instructor since 2015. Developed and led an Inspector Development Program and is recognized for mentoring emerging inspectors and focusing on building a strong next generation of code officials. Uses personal professional experiences, including the CBO exam journey, to strengthen instruction and mentoring.
achievements_and_justification.additional_comments_on_why_the_nominee_s:
Michael Harrington has demonstrated sustained leadership and service at the chapter, state, regional, and national levels of the code enforcement profession. His roles in the Metro Atlanta BOAG Chapter (Treasurer, Vice President, President, Board Member Emeritus) and in BOAG (Board Director, Vice President, President) show a long-term commitment to advancing building safety in Georgia. Through ICC Region VIII service as Georgia Representative and Vice Chair, and as a member of the ICC Code Development Committee and an ICC instructor since 2015, he has contributed to code development and education well beyond his local jurisdiction.

He is recognized for ethical integrity under pressure, including his handling of the Oak Commons Medical Plaza project, where he documented serious life-safety issues and was prepared to recommend a stop-work order despite known political connections between a councilmember and the developer, explicitly committing to route any political contact through proper channels. He has used such experiences, along with his near-burnout after the Town Center Phase II work session, as real-world examples in BOAG ethics and mentoring discussions.

Michael has invested heavily in mentoring and legacy-building, developing an Inspector Development Program, coaching younger inspectors through ethics issues, and using his own professional challenges to improve how he teaches and supports others. His civic involvement with the Peachtree Ridge CERT program, Habitat for Humanity in Gwinnett County, and the Peachtree Ridge High School CTE Advisory Board reflects a broader commitment to public safety and to preparing future professionals. Taken together, his leadership, ethical conduct, mentoring, and community service exemplify the spirit of the Hal Cosper Memorial Lifetime Achievement Award.
